{{AI-generated|date=December 2025|reason=Text added in [[Special:Diff/1259235637|this 2024 edit]]; see [[WP:AISIGNS]] sections on AI vocabulary, superficial analyses/original research introduced therein, etc. Note also similar indicators in [[Special:Diff/1254334668|sandbox content]] related to that edit.}}
{{Short description|Snubbing a person for a mobile phone}}{{Redirect|Phub|the pornographic website known colloqually as "Phub"|Pornhub}}[[File:Text hug (16232940323).jpg|thumb|Detail of [[Paul Day (sculptor)|Paul Day]]'s 2007 sculpture ''[[The Meeting Place (sculpture)|The Meeting Place]]'', showing a woman looking at her mobile phone while in an embrace]]'''Phubbing''' refers to the act of ignoring one's immediate social interactions in favor of engaging with a [[smartphone]], making it a prevalent behavior in today's digital age. While it is frequently associated with the concept of fear of missing out ([[Fear of missing out|FOMO]]), the current body of research presents conflicting evidence regarding the strength and consistency of this relationship.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Ansari |first1=Sameer |last2=Azeem |first2=Ahmad |last3=Khan |first3=Irum |last4=Iqbal |first4=Naved |date=2024-07-01 |title=Association of Phubbing Behavior and Fear of Missing Out: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis |url=https://www.liebertpub.com/doi/10.1089/cyber.2023.0761 |journal=Cyberpsychology, Behavior, and Social Networking |language=en |volume=27 |issue=7 |pages=467–481 |doi=10.1089/cyber.2023.0761 |pmid=38757677 |issn=2152-2715|url-access=subscription }}</ref> In May 2012, as part of a linguistic experiment by ''[[Macquarie Dictionary]]'', the advertising agency behind the campaign, [[McCann (company)|McCann]], had invited a number of lexicographers, authors, and poets to coin a [[neologism]] to describe the behavior. The word "phubbing," a [[portmanteau]] of ''[[phone]]'' and ''[[Snub|snubbing]]'', was first described by McCann Group Account Director Adrian Mills, who was working with [[David Astle]]. The term has appeared in media around the world and was popularized by the Stop Phubbing campaign created by McCann.<ref>{{Cite AV media |url=https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-mbTR2IYAfo&feature=youtu.be |title=The First Use of the Word Phubbing |date=2025-02-03 |last=Adrian Mills |access-date=2025-02-03 |via=YouTube}}</ref>

Despite its growing relevance, no standardized method exists to measure phubbing behaviors or their effects. Existing tools have been developed with limited attention to psychometric properties or broad applicability, creating challenges for advancing research in this area.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Kahneman |first1=Daniel |last2=Krueger |first2=Alan B. |last3=Schkade |first3=David A. |last4=Schwarz |first4=Norbert |last5=Stone |first5=Arthur A. |date=2004-12-03 |title=A Survey Method for Characterizing Daily Life Experience: The Day Reconstruction Method |url=https://www.science.org/doi/10.1126/science.1103572 |journal=Science |language=en |volume=306 |issue=5702 |pages=1776–1780 |doi=10.1126/science.1103572 |pmid=15576620 |bibcode=2004Sci...306.1776K |issn=0036-8075|url-access=subscription }}</ref> To address these gaps, recent studies aim to create validated scales that capture the complex dimensions of phubbing, contributing to a more comprehensive understanding of its impact on social interactions and individual well-being.

== How phubbing manifests ==
Phubbing typically manifests in various social contexts where individuals prioritize their smartphones over face-to-face interactions, leading to a breakdown in meaningful communication. It has become increasingly common to observe groups of friends engrossed in their devices, couples at restaurants ignoring one another while staring at their screens, or families spending time together yet disconnected by their preoccupation with smartphones. This behavior is not confined to personal settings; it extends to workplaces, where professionals often divert their attention from colleagues to their devices. Over time, phubbing has evolved into a socially accepted norm, undermining the quality of interpersonal relationships and healthy communication.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Sun |first1=Juhyung |last2=Yoon |first2=Doyle |date=2023-12-14 |editor-last=Fashoto |editor-first=Stephen Gbenga |title=Not My Fault to Phub Friends! Individual, Social, and Technological Influences on Phubbing and Its Consequences |journal=Human Behavior and Emerging Technologies |language=en |volume=2023 |pages=1–12 |doi=10.1155/2023/4059010 |doi-access=free |issn=2578-1863}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Garrido |first1=Estefanía Capilla |last2=Delgado |first2=Sixto Cubo |last3=Esteban |first3=Prudencia Gutiérrez |date=2024-08-01 |title=Phubbing and its impact on the individual's psychological well-being |journal=Acta Psychologica |volume=248 |article-number=104388 |doi=10.1016/j.actpsy.2024.104388 |issn=0001-6918|doi-access=free |pmid=38959636 }}</ref>

Furthermore, the habitual nature of phubbing reinforces its cyclical impact: as individuals use their phones to escape stress or loneliness, they inadvertently weaken their relationships, leading to greater isolation and reliance on their devices. This pattern not only exacerbates psychological challenges such as [[anxiety]] and [[Depression (mood)|depression]] but also raises broader concerns about how technology shapes social norms and values. Addressing these issues requires a deeper understanding of the motivations behind phubbing and its effects on mental health and social connections. Efforts to mitigate phubbing's impact must balance the benefits of technology with the need for meaningful, face-to-face interactions to preserve the integrity of human relationships.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Cao |first1=Xiongfei |last2=Masood |first2=Ayesha |last3=Luqman |first3=Adeel |last4=Ali |first4=Ahmed |date=2018-08-01 |title=Excessive use of mobile social networking sites and poor academic performance: Antecedents and consequences from stressor-strain-outcome perspective |url=https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0747563218301286 |journal=Computers in Human Behavior |volume=85 |pages=163–174 |doi=10.1016/j.chb.2018.03.023 |issn=0747-5632|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

== Phubbing and technology ==
The pervasive design of app [[Notification service|notifications]], [[Algorithm|algorithms]], and [[User interface|user interfaces]] plays a pivotal role in fostering phubbing behavior. These features are engineered to capture user attention, often at the expense of in-person interactions. Central to this dynamic is smart push technology, which delivers personalized, real-time updates to users. While this technology enhances convenience, it also amplifies [[Fear of missing out|Fear of Missing Out]] (FoMO), a psychological phenomenon defined as the anxiety of being excluded from rewarding experiences.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Przybylski |first1=Andrew K. |last2=Murayama |first2=Kou |last3=DeHaan |first3=Cody R. |last4=Gladwell |first4=Valerie |date=2013-07-01 |title=Motivational, emotional, and behavioral correlates of fear of missing out |url=https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0747563213000800 |journal=Computers in Human Behavior |volume=29 |issue=4 |pages=1841–1848 |doi=10.1016/j.chb.2013.02.014 |issn=0747-5632|url-access=subscription }}</ref> By continuously pushing tailored content, apps entice users to engage compulsively, distracting them from their immediate social environment.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Alabri |first=Amna |date=2022-02-16 |editor-last=Yan |editor-first=Zheng |title=Fear of Missing Out (FOMO): The Effects of the Need to Belong, Perceived Centrality, and Fear of Social Exclusion |journal=Human Behavior and Emerging Technologies |language=en |volume=2022 |pages=1–12 |doi=10.1155/2022/4824256 |doi-access=free |issn=2578-1863}}</ref>

The timeliness of content delivery, a hallmark of [[Push technology|push notifications]], adds another layer of complexity. Constant updates induce information overload, leaving users overwhelmed yet compelled to stay updated. This creates a cognitive loop where users feel pressured to remain engaged, even at the cost of neglecting their physical surroundings.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Schmuck |first=Desirée |date=2021-09-18 |title=Following Social Media Influencers in Early Adolescence: Fear of Missing Out, Social Well-Being and Supportive Communication with Parents |url=https://academic.oup.com/jcmc/article/26/5/245/6372361 |journal=Journal of Computer-Mediated Communication |language=en |volume=26 |issue=5 |pages=245–264 |doi=10.1093/jcmc/zmab008 |issn=1083-6101|doi-access=free }}</ref> Over time, this cycle fosters a dependency on smartphones, making phubbing a habitual behavior.

== Parental phubbing and its effects on children ==
Parental phubbing, the act of parents prioritizing smartphone use over engagement with their children, poses a significant threat to children's social-emotional development. This behavior disrupts parent-child interactions, often leading to feelings of neglect and reduced emotional connection for children.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Lin |first1=Chen-xi |last2=Qing |first2=Zai-hua |last3=Yan |first3=Chu-han |last4=Tang |first4=Shi-jiao |last5=Wang |first5=Cheng-lei |last6=Yang |first6=Xin-hua |last7=Liu |first7=Xiao-qun |date=2024-07-08 |title=The Relationship Between Parental Phubbing and Adolescents' Depressive Symptoms: a Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis |url=https://link.springer.com/10.1007/s40894-024-00244-0 |journal=Adolescent Research Review |volume=10 |pages=19–29 |language=en |doi=10.1007/s40894-024-00244-0 |issn=2363-8346|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

A study by Liu et al.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Niu |first1=Gengfeng |last2=Yao |first2=Liangshuang |last3=Wu |first3=Li |last4=Tian |first4=Yuan |last5=Xu |first5=Lei |last6=Sun |first6=Xiaojun |date=2020-09-01 |title=Parental phubbing and adolescent problematic mobile phone use: The role of parent-child relationship and self-control |url=https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0190740920305272 |journal=Children and Youth Services Review |volume=116 |article-number=105247 |doi=10.1016/j.childyouth.2020.105247 |issn=0190-7409|url-access=subscription }}</ref>  involving 726 adolescents aged 12–18 revealed that parental phubbing significantly disrupted the parent-child relationship, which in turn led to higher levels of mobile phone addiction. The study found that 51.45% of the adolescents experienced frequent parental phubbing, such as during mealtimes, which contributed to feelings of neglect and rejection. These interactions were linked to heightened rates of behavioral issues, including depression and aggression, with phubbing explaining 26% of the variance in problematic mobile phone use (β = 0.26, ''p'' < 0.001).

These findings resonate with the ecological techno-subsystem theory introduced by Johnson and Puplampu, which asserts that modern technologies interfere with family systems and individual development.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Johnson |first1=Genevieve Marie |last2=Puplampu |first2=Korbla P. |date=2008 |title=Internet Use during Childhood and the Ecological Techno-Subsystem |url=https://eric.ed.gov/?id=EJ1073829 |journal=Canadian Journal of Learning and Technology |language=en |volume=34 |issue=1 |doi=10.21432/T2CP4T |issn=1499-6677|doi-access=free }}</ref> Parental phubbing not only disrupts immediate interactions but also contributes to broader behavioral issues, such as increased aggression and loneliness in adolescents.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Chotpitayasunondh |first1=Varoth |last2=Douglas |first2=Karen M. |date=2016-10-01 |title=How "phubbing" becomes the norm: The antecedents and consequences of snubbing via smartphone |journal=Computers in Human Behavior |volume=63 |pages=9–18 |doi=10.1016/j.chb.2016.05.018 |issn=0747-5632|doi-access=free }}</ref> This underscores the importance of addressing phubbing behaviors within families. Effective interventions could focus on reducing parental screen time,<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Kuss |first1=Daria J. |last2=Kanjo |first2=Eiman |last3=Crook-Rumsey |first3=Mark |last4=Kibowski |first4=Fraenze |last5=Wang |first5=Grace Y. |last6=Sumich |first6=Alex |date=September 2018 |title=Problematic Mobile Phone Use and Addiction Across Generations: the Roles of Psychopathological Symptoms and Smartphone Use |journal=Journal of Technology in Behavioral Science |language=en |volume=3 |issue=3 |pages=141–149 |doi=10.1007/s41347-017-0041-3 |issn=2366-5963 |pmc=6133055 |pmid=30238057}}</ref> reinforcing quality interactions, and fostering resilience in adolescents to counteract the detrimental effects of digital distractions on familial relationships.

== Research on phubbing and its relational effects ==
In October 2015, media outlets (such as ''[[Today (U.S. TV program)|Today]]''<ref name="Today">{{cite web |date=October 1, 2015 |title=Does your partner love his cellphone more than you? Take this survey |url=http://www.today.com/health/does-he-love-his-cellphone-more-you-survey-t47046 |access-date=October 4, 2015 |publisher=TODAY |vauthors=Holohan M}}</ref> and [[Digital Trends]]<ref name="Digital">{{cite web |date=October 3, 2015 |title=What is phubbing, and is it ruining your relationships? |url=http://www.digitaltrends.com/mobile/what-is-phubbing-and-is-it-ruining-your-relationships/ |access-date=October 4, 2015 |publisher=Digital Trends |vauthors=Chang L}}</ref>) reported on a study by James A. Roberts, professor of marketing at [[Baylor University]] Hankamer School of Business, that was published in the journal ''[[Computers in Human Behavior|Computers In Human Behavior]]''. The study consisted of two separate surveys of more than 450 U.S. adults to learn the relational effects of "phubbing" or partner phubbing. The survey found that 46.3 percent of respondents said their partners phubbed them, and 22.6 percent said it caused issues in their relationship.<ref name="Today" /> In an interview with [[Yahoo!]] Health, Roberts said, "We found that the ones that reported higher partner phubbing fought more with their partner and were less satisfied with their relationship than those who reported less phubbing."<ref name="Digital" />

== Boss phubbing ==
Boss phubbing (BPhubbing) refers to the behavior of supervisors being distracted by their smartphones during interactions with employees. This emerging workplace phenomenon has gained attention due to its negative impact on employee trust, engagement, and overall job performance. Recent research has sought to explore the underlying psychological and relational mechanisms through which BPhubbing affects workplace dynamics.<ref>{{Cite book |url=https://academic.oup.com/edited-volume/34869 |title=The Oxford Handbook of Mobile Communication and Society |date=2020-04-02 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-086438-5 |editor-last=Ling |editor-first=Rich |edition=1 |language=en |doi=10.1093/oxfordhb/9780190864385.001.0001 |editor-last2=Fortunati |editor-first2=Leopoldina |editor-last3=Goggin |editor-first3=Gerard |editor-last4=Lim |editor-first4=Sun Sun |editor-last5=Li |editor-first5=Yuling}}</ref>

A series of studies examined the effects of BPhubbing on employee engagement, grounded in theories like Reciprocated Social Exchange (RSE) and Kahn's psychological conditions.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Li |first1=Alex Ning |last2=Tan |first2=Hwee Hoon |date=April 2013 |title=What happens when you trust your supervisor? Mediators of individual performance in trust relationships |url=https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/job.1812 |journal=Journal of Organizational Behavior |language=en |volume=34 |issue=3 |pages=407–425 |doi=10.1002/job.1812 |issn=0894-3796|url-access=subscription }}</ref> These studies found that BPhubbing erodes trust between supervisors and employees, which is a cornerstone of healthy workplace relationships. Supervisory trust was identified as a mediator, linking BPhubbing to lower employee engagement through diminished psychological meaningfulness and availability.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Li |first1=Alex Ning |last2=Tan |first2=Hwee Hoon |date=April 2013 |title=What happens when you trust your supervisor? Mediators of individual performance in trust relationships |url=https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/job.1812 |journal=Journal of Organizational Behavior |language=en |volume=34 |issue=3 |pages=407–425 |doi=10.1002/job.1812 |issn=0894-3796|url-access=subscription }}</ref> Employees who experienced BPhubbing were less likely to find their work valuable or believe in their ability to perform effectively, leading to decreased motivation and productivity.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Schneider |first1=Stefan |last2=Stone |first2=Arthur A. |date=2016 |title=Ambulatory and diary methods can facilitate the measurement of patient-reported outcomes |journal=Quality of Life Research |volume=25 |issue=3 |pages=497–506 |doi=10.1007/s11136-015-1054-z |jstor=44852369 |pmid=26101141 |pmc=4689672 |issn=0962-9343}}</ref>

Interestingly, the research by James A. Roberts and Meredith E. David<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Roberts |first1=James A. |last2=David |first2=Meredith E. |date=2017-10-01 |title=Put down your phone and listen to me: How boss phubbing undermines the psychological conditions necessary for employee engagement |url=https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0747563217303333 |journal=Computers in Human Behavior |volume=75 |pages=206–217 |doi=10.1016/j.chb.2017.05.021 |issn=0747-5632|url-access=subscription }}</ref> revealed that psychological safety, another factor traditionally associated with engagement, did not significantly mediate the relationship between supervisory trust and engagement in the context of BPhubbing. This highlights the complex and context-dependent nature of the psychological conditions driving employee behavior. The findings suggest that reducing BPhubbing could have profound implications for organizational outcomes. Practical recommendations include implementing training programs for supervisors to increase awareness of the impact of their smartphone use, establishing clear smartphone usage policies, and fostering a corporate culture that values attentiveness and interpersonal connection.

== Phubbing in partners ==
Partner Phubbing, defined as the "extent to which your romantic partner uses or is distracted by his/her cell phone while in your company" , may be a social allergy that causes the affected romantic partner to respond negatively.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Roberts |first1=James A. |last2=David |first2=Meredith E. |date=2022 |title=Partner Phubbing as a Social Allergen: Support for a Dual Process Model |journal=Human Behavior and Emerging Technologies |language=en |volume=2022 |issue=1 |article-number=6432445 |doi=10.1155/2022/6432445 |doi-access=free |issn=2578-1863}}</ref> This causes difficulties because partners feel neglected when their significant other favors phone calls over face-to-face interactions.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Valkenburg |first1=Patti M. |last2=Peter |first2=Jochen |date=July 2007 |title=Online Communication and Adolescent Well-Being: Testing the Stimulation Versus the Displacement Hypothesis |url=https://academic.oup.com/jcmc/article/12/4/1169-1182/4582968 |journal=Journal of Computer-Mediated Communication |language=en |volume=12 |issue=4 |pages=1169–1182 |doi=10.1111/j.1083-6101.2007.00368.x|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

Studies show that 25% of married couples and 42% of unmarried couples in serious relationships report feeling distracted by their partner's phone use. This distraction can erode intimacy, trust, and overall relationship satisfaction.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Przybylski |first1=Andrew K. |last2=Weinstein |first2=Netta |date=2013-05-01 |title=Can you connect with me now? How the presence of mobile communication technology influences face-to-face conversation quality |url=https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/0265407512453827 |journal=Journal of Social and Personal Relationships |language=en |volume=30 |issue=3 |pages=237–246 |doi=10.1177/0265407512453827 |issn=0265-4075|url-access=subscription }}</ref> When one partner engages in phubbing, it indicates a lack of focus, leaving the other feeling undervalued or rejected.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Elphinston |first1=Rachel A. |last2=Noller |first2=Patricia |date=November 2011 |title=Time to Face It! Facebook Intrusion and the Implications for Romantic Jealousy and Relationship Satisfaction |url=http://www.liebertpub.com/doi/10.1089/cyber.2010.0318 |journal=Cyberpsychology, Behavior, and Social Networking |language=en |volume=14 |issue=11 |pages=631–635 |doi=10.1089/cyber.2010.0318 |pmid=21548798 |issn=2152-2715|url-access=subscription }}</ref> This dynamic is especially harmful in established partnerships, when there are higher expectations of emotional support and response. In longer relationships, partners are more interdependent, meaning that one person's behavior (like excessive phone use) is more likely to affect the other's emotional state.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=McDaniel |first1=Brandon T. |last2=Coyne |first2=Sarah M. |date=January 2016 |title="Technoference": The interference of technology in couple relationships and implications for women's personal and relational well-being. |url=https://doi.apa.org/doi/10.1037/ppm0000065 |journal=Psychology of Popular Media Culture |language=en |volume=5 |issue=1 |pages=85–98 |doi=10.1037/ppm0000065 |issn=2160-4142|url-access=subscription }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors=((Frackowiak, M.)), ((Hilpert, P.)), ((Russell, P. S.)) | journal=Current Psychology | title=Impact of partner phubbing on negative emotions: a daily diary study of mitigating factors | date=2024 | volume=43 | issue=2 | pages=1835–1854 | doi=10.1007/s12144-023-04401-x}}</ref> In contrast, shorter-term relationships may not experience these effects as strongly because the emotional investment and expectations are still developing. More recent research indicates that co-present phone use in daily relational interactions may be integrated into couple's activities, without necessary provoking any negative sentiment or feeling ignored.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors=((Frackowiak, M.)), ((Russell, P. S.)), ((Vowels, M. J.)), ((Hilpert, P.)) | journal=Mobile Media & Communication | title=Disentangling the good, the bad, and the neutral of co-present mobile phone use: A new perspective on "partner phubbing" | date=2025 | volume=13 | issue=2 | pages=295–318 | doi=10.1177/20501579241298271| doi-access=free }}</ref>

The role of digital media in relationship conflicts adds another layer to the issue. Texting and social media use can trigger misunderstandings, reduce intimacy, and even serve as an escape from relationship problems.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=David |first1=Meredith E. |last2=Roberts |first2=James A. |date=December 2021 |title=Investigating the impact of partner phubbing on romantic jealousy and relationship satisfaction: The moderating role of attachment anxiety |url=https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/0265407521996454 |journal=Journal of Social and Personal Relationships |language=en |volume=38 |issue=12 |pages=3590–3609 |doi=10.1177/0265407521996454 |issn=0265-4075|url-access=subscription }}</ref> Mood management theory<ref>{{Cite journal |last=ZILLMANN |first=DOLF |date=1988-01-01 |title=Mood Management Through Communication Choices |url=https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/000276488031003005 |journal=American Behavioral Scientist |language=en |volume=31 |issue=3 |pages=327–340 |doi=10.1177/000276488031003005 |issn=0002-7642|url-access=subscription }}</ref> suggests that individuals turn to digital interactions to improve their emotional state when dealing with discomfort in their relationships. Therefore, excessive phone use may not only weaken relationship quality (the negative effect hypothesis) but also be a symptom of an already strained relationship, where partners seek relief through digital distraction.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=McDaniel |first1=Brandon T. |last2=Galovan |first2=Adam M. |last3=Cravens |first3=Jaclyn D. |last4=Drouin |first4=Michelle |date=March 2018 |title="Technoference" and implications for mothers' and fathers' couple and coparenting relationship quality |journal=Computers in Human Behavior |language=en |volume=80 |pages=303–313 |doi=10.1016/j.chb.2017.11.019 |pmc=6812535 |pmid=31649418}}</ref>
